titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ann Lauterbach Papers
titlestmt.author: by Beinecke staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 4701221.
This collection contains 3 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 106 digital files.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday December 5, 2024 at 23:03
Transformed with yale.addEadidUrl.xsl. Adds @url with handle for finding aid. Overwrites @url if already present.
Finding aid revised by Janet Lopes to describe May 2022 acquisition.

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Ann Lauterbach Papers document the personal and professional life of American poet Ann Lauterbach and consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, born-digital and audiovisual material. The papers contain a number of drafts of Lauterbach's writings from early in her career until roughly 2000. Her correspondence reflects her intellectual circle with correspondents including writers and artists: Stacy Doris, Kenward Gray Elmslie, Richard Hamilton, Susan Howe, Garrett Kalleberg, Ron Padgett, Peter Straub, Marjorie Welish, and Chet Wiener. The papers also contain material relating to Lauterbach's family members including her father, war correspondent Richard Lauterbach.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.
Boxes 29-30 (audiovisual material): Use of originals is restricted. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 85 (student records): Restricted until 2090. For further information consult the appropriate curator.
Box 129 (born-digital): Restricted fragile. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from George Robert Minkoff Inc. on the Eugene G. O'Neill Memorial Fund, 2001, Granary Books, Inc. on the Elizabeth Wakeman Dwight Memorial Fund, 2007, and Granary Books on the Ruth Stephan Fund and the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three groupings: I. February 2001 acquisition, 1926-1999. II. May 2007 acquisition, 1970-2000. III. May 2022 acquisition, 1918-2020.

bioghist.head: Ann Lauterbach (1942- )
bioghist.p: American poet Ann Lauterbach was born on September 28, 1942, in New York City to Richard Edward (a war correspondent) and Elisabeth Stuart Wardewell Lauterbach. She graduated from the High School of Music and Art in New York City and then obtained a Bachelor of Arts from the University of Wisconsin in 1964. She attended Columbia University on a Woodrow Wilson graduate fellowship from 1966 to 1967.

Following her studies, Lauterbach moved to London, England, where she served as an editor for Thames & Hudson Publishers (1967-1973) and taught at Saint Martin's School of Art (1967-1973). During this time Lauterbach was also director of the literature program at the Institute of Contemporary Art. While living in England Lauterbach published her first book of poetry: Vertical, Horizontal (Dublin, Ireland: Seafront Press, 1971).

Lauterbach returned to New York City where she was employed as an art consultant for various galleries (1974-1984) and as the assistant director of the Washburn Gallery (1984-1986). Lauterbach has maintained a career teaching creative writing concurrently with writing and publishing her poetry. She served as a professor of creative writing at various universities, including Brooklyn College (1985-1986), City College, City University of New York, (1989-1998), and the Graduate Center of the University of New York (1993). In 1991 Lauterbach joined the M.F.A. writing faculty at Bard College.

Her poetry publications include: Book One (Spring Street Press: New York, NY, 1975), Clamor (Viking: New York, NY, 1991), And for Example (Viking: New York, NY, 1994), and Or to Begin Again (Penguin Books: New York, NY, 2009). Lauterbach also published Greeks with Bruce Boice (Hollow Press: Baltimore, MD, 1985) and an essay The Night Sky: Writings on the Poetics of Experience (Viking: New York, NY, 2005). She has contributed to a number of periodicals including American Letters and Commentary , American Poetry Review , Artforum , and Denver Quarterly .

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ann Lauterbach Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and in some instances minimal organization. Various acquisitions associated with the collection have not been merged and organized as a whole. Each acquisition is described separately in the contents list below, identified by a unique call number and titled according to month and year of acquisition.

The finding aid for this collection is compiled from individual preliminary lists for each acquisition that were created at or around the time of receipt by the library. The preliminary lists were migrated to comply with current archival descriptive standards and merged into a single file in 2007-2008. As part of the migration, modifications were made to the formatting of individual lists; however, the content of the lists was neither modified nor verified.

As a rule, descriptive information found in the Collection Contents section is drawn in large part from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
Former call numbers: Uncat MSS 109 and Uncat MSS 943.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Ann Lauterbach Papers document the personal and professional life of American poet Ann Lauterbach and consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, and audiovisual material.

Lauterbach's creative life is reflected in her writings and correspondence. The papers contain a number of drafts of Lauterbach's writings from early in her career until roughly 2000. Her correspondence reflects her intellectual circle with correspondents including a number of writers and artists: Stacy Doris, Kenward Gray Elmslie, Richard Hamilton, Susan Howe, Garrett Kalleberg, Ron Padgett, Peter Straub, Marjorie Welish, and Chet Wiener. The papers also contain other authors' manuscripts submitted to Lauterbach for review.

In addition, the papers relate to Lauterbach's personal life. Included in the papers are journals and yearly planners dating from the 1960s to the 1990s. Lauterbach's correspondence with family members, namely her mother (Elizabeth Lauterbach) and sister (Jennifer Lauterbach), similarly provide insight into her life. The papers also include a journal belonging to her father Richard Lauterbach.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Ann Lauterbach Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: February 2001 Acquisition is organized into seven groupings: Correspondence, Poetry, Prose, Journals, Early Writing, Ephemera, and Oversize.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains: correspondence, writings, printed material, and audiovisual material by or relating to Ann Lauterbach. Includes Greeks drafts and proofs.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 30 (audiocassettes): Use of originals is restricted. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

did.unittitle: Book cover for "These are the Russians," by Richard Lauterbach
"Many Times, But Then," Univ. of Texas Press Poetry Series
Drafts of "MTBT" and "Book One," and "Ideas of the Actual"
"A Clown, Some Colors, A Doll, Her Stories, A Song, a Moonlit Cove," with letters
"On a Stair" galley book with author's corrections
"Later that Evening," pub. Jordan Davis, NY
"A Clown, some colors, doll, her stories, a song, a moonlit cove" with Ellen Phelan
Ann's essays--West magazine, "Use this word in a sentence," poems
Drafts of "Lines written to Bob Perelman in the margins of Marginalization of Poetry"
"Pragmatic Example," "How I think about what I write"
Memoirs of Stuyvesant's Folly, English class work, stories, essays
Charles Altieri, "Jorie Graham and Ann Lauterbach: Towards a Contemporary Poetics of Eloquence" Cream City review
Charles Altieri, "Ann Lauterbach's Still," Wallace Stevens Journal
Garrett Kalleberg, "A Form of Duration: On Ann Lauterbach's 'And for Example,'" Denver Quarterly
"River Decisions," AL at C.U.N.Y., Bobbelmann, Duncan
Unpublished essays on AL--Anne Shaw, Gweneth Catlin, Steven Mounkhall
Janet Bowdan, "Diverse strangers flower and diverge, and when they cross paths three strangers flower and are clairvoyant: Three Reviews", review of "Clamor"
William Corbett, "Scrapple from the Apple: A Charlie Parker tune," Harvard review
Krysia Jopek, "Prepositional Investigations of Language and Poetry"
"Canon, Temple, Living within the Pause," review of "And for Example," Claudia Keelan
Review of "On a Stair," Andrew McCarron
"Eternal Questions, Unmatched Beauty," review of "On a Stair," Dionisio Martinez
"Stair Master: AL Traces the Mind in Motion," John Olson
Review of all books by AL
Donald Revell, "Rose as Decoy, Beauty as Use: Mark Rudman, David St. John, AL"
Donald Revell, "The Plural of Vision Remains Vision:...AL"
Leonard Schwartz, "La Crete du Neuf: Nouvelle Poesie Americaine"
"Process and Movement," review of "Clamor," Juliana Spahr
Henry Taylor, "Ordinary Words, Extraordinary meanings: 3 Poets"
"Spoken Words: The Spaces Between the World and its Telling," Syd M.
"Sound and Sense: Review of Clamor," Nicholas Christopher and Harriet Zinnes
"Links w/out Links: The Voice of the Turtle"
"A Life in Soho, When Even its Name was New" AL essay
"Is I Another? A Talk in 7 Beginnings"
"Slaves of Fashion" response to Harold Bloom
"Lines written to Bob Perelman in the margins of 'The Marginalization of Poetry'"
Barbara Guest, American letters and commentary, "Unpicturing fair realism"
Genesis, "Communion: Contemporary writers reveal the Bible in their lives"
"What is the grass: Notes leading up to and away from W. Whitman"
"Fifteen ways of preparing to think about language as place in urban poetics"
"On flaws: towards a poetics of the whole fragment"
Conversation between Heather Ramsdell and AL
"Genius in Exile," Interview with Joseph Vogue
Introduction of AL by Beth Anderson
Intro of and by AL in D.C.
Intro for Susan Howe at DIA
Art reviews--Donal Sultan and Joel Shapiro
Essay or poetry on Dylan Thomas

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: There are two audiocassettes related to this material that can be found in Box 30. See: Brodsky/Lauterbach: Side One/Starts in middle; Side 2 - All (1 of 2 cassettes) Brodsky/Lauterbach: Side 3 - all; check Side 4 (2 of 2 cassettes)

did.unittitle: Greeks. Original layout and art work

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: May 2007 Acquisition is organized into five groupings: Resumes, Texts of Early Interviews, Projects and Correspondence 1970-1999; Project and Correspondence Files 1999-2000 and Visual Artists Files; Manuscripts of Other Authors; Other Papers; and Audiovisual Media.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains: correspondence, writings, printed material, videocassettes, and other papers.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 29 (videocassettes): Use of originals is restricted. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchase from Granary Books on the Ruth Stephan Fund and the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six groupings: Writings, 1954-2019; Works by others, 1941-2020; Teaching materials, 1961-2020; Journals, 1973-2019; Correspondence, 1950-2020; and Photographic prints, 1918-2002.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This accession contains writings, manuscripts, teaching materials, correspondence, ephemera, photographic prints, and computer media.
